items.front: Who should receive beacon code assignments?
items.back: Assign beacon codes only to ADS-B and/or transponder-equipped aircraft, normally using computer-assigned codes. Use code 4000 for flights not compatible with a discrete code assignment.

items.front: When may you request a radar beacon code change after a handoff?
items.back: Do not request a change from the transferring facility's code until the aircraft is within your area, unless a directive, LOA, or specific coordination allows earlier.

items.front: What code is assigned to an emergency aircraft that is not radar identified?
items.back: Assign code 7700. After radio and radar contact are established, you may change most aircraft to a discrete code when conditions and pilot concurrence allow.

items.front: What does a code 7600 display require you to do?
items.back: Apply communications-failure procedures.

items.front: What does a code 7500 display require you to do?
items.back: Apply hijack or unlawful-interference procedures.

items.front: What is the controller's job when a UAS displays code 7400?
items.back: Determine the lost-link procedure, coordinate as needed, and support the programmed lost-link route and profile because the pilot no longer controls the aircraft normally.

items.front: What code should a VFR aircraft use when radar advisory service ends?
items.back: Instruct the aircraft to squawk VFR or 1200. VFR aircraft receiving radar advisories should have a computer-assigned beacon code while the service is being provided.

items.front: How are pressure-suit flights and flights above FL600 normally handled on beacon code?
items.back: Keep them on the reserved special-use code filed in the flight plan. Change, standby, or stop-squawk instructions are used only if same-code conflicts make them necessary.

items.front: What code rule applies to exercise FAKER aircraft?
items.back: Ensure they remain on the exercise discrete beacon code filed in the flight plan.

items.front: When may you instruct an aircraft to squawk standby?
items.back: Use standby only when the aircraft is about 15 miles from destination and you no longer need the transponder or ADS-B, or when needed to reduce clutter, then return it to normal as soon as practical.

items.front: Which codes must be continuously monitored in normal code-monitor duty?
items.back: Monitor assigned codes plus 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 unless your area is only Class A. Monitor 4000 and any substitute code when nearby special-use operations require it.

items.front: What do you do when an aircraft is not displaying its assigned code or the transponder appears failed?
items.back: Tell the pilot to reset the transponder and squawk the assigned code, and notify the next control position or facility if the transponder is inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.front: What must you tell aircraft when the beacon interrogator appears inoperative or malfunctioning?
items.back: Advise the affected aircraft that the beacon interrogator is inoperative or malfunctioning.

items.front: When may you disapprove or withdraw approval to operate with a failed transponder or ADS-B Out transmitter?
items.back: Only on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.

items.front: When must Mode C altitude readout be validated?
items.back: Validate it after initial track start, track start from coast or frozen status, during and after an unreliable readout, and after interfacility handoff except for the listed tower or TRACON exceptions.

items.front: When is assigned altitude verification waived for Mode C aircraft on initial contact?
items.back: It is waived if the pilot states the altitude, you assign a new one while the aircraft is climbing or descending, valid Mode C shows established at it, or the terminal intrafacility exception applies.

items.front: What altitude-confirmation rule applies to non-Mode C aircraft?
items.back: On initial contact, verify assigned altitude unless a listed exception applies. In the USA, reconfirm all pilot altitude readbacks.

items.front: How do you control automatic altitude reporting?
items.back: Tell the pilot when altitude reporting should be on or off by issuing SQUAWK ALTITUDE or STOP ALTITUDE SQUAWK.

items.front: When may an inflight deviation from transponder or Mode C requirements be approved?
items.back: Between 10,000 and 18,000 feet MSL in the contiguous U.S., do not approve an inflight deviation for aircraft without installed equipment except in an emergency.

items.front: How is beacon termination accomplished?
items.back: Instruct the pilot to STOP SQUAWK. For military aircraft, specify the mode if needed.

items.front: How should terminal altitude filters be set?
items.back: Set filters to cover all altitudes in the controller's jurisdiction, with the upper limit at least 1,000 feet above the highest altitude responsibility.

items.front: What must be done when an aircraft's ADS-B transmitter appears inoperative or malfunctioning?
items.back: Notify the OS/CIC of the call sign, location, and UTC time. Inform the pilot except for DoD or authorized OFF operations, and stop transmissions if the malfunction affects safe ATC operations.

items.front: What triggers a CSMM ADS-B alert and what do you do with it?
items.back: It occurs when the transmitted ADS-B flight ID does not match the flight plan aircraft identification, and you inform the aircraft of the mismatch.

items.front: What do you do if a pilot requests ADS-B Out OFF operations?
items.back: Do not approve the request. Notify the OS/CIC with the aircraft call sign and location, and do not tell an authorized OFF aircraft that its ADS-B appears inoperative.
